text:
Centre for Epilepsy

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Emma K. Shah 
Consultant Neurologist  
Centre for Epilepsy  
Kingston Hospital, Galsworthy Road, Kingston upon Thames KT2 7QB  

Dear Dr Emma K. Shah, 

Mr Daniel Harper, DOB: 22-09-1991, Hospital No: K8429173 NHS No. 9432015876  
Flat 12, 5 Riverside Court, Kingston upon Thames, KT1 1AB  

Diagnosis: 

Recurrent unprovoked seizures; classification not established

Aetiology: 

Undetermined; no focal neurological deficit on examination, MRI brain (2024) unremarkable; EEG nonspecific background changes

Other Medical Problems: 

History of migraine without aura, currently intermittent and self-managed with simple analgesia

Management Plan: 

- Medication: Continue Lamotrigine 100 mg twice daily as current baseline therapy. Add Levetiracetam 250 mg twice daily for two weeks, then increase to 500 mg twice daily if tolerated. Previously trialled Topiramate (discontinued due to cognitive slowing) and Carbamazepine (stopped due to rash). Avoid Topiramate given migraine history unless benefits outweigh cognitive effects.  
- Monitoring: Seizure diary shows two cluster days this month; sizes unrecorded. Patient will continue to document dates, any precipitating factors, timing relative to missed doses, and recovery features.  
- Safety/Driving: Discussed standard safety advice around bathing, heights, and operating machinery. Advised to follow DVLA guidance regarding seizures and driving; patient reports not currently driving.  
- Investigations: Serum Lamotrigine trough level in 4 weeks after dose stability; U&E, LFTs, FBC at the same time. Consider ambulatory EEG if clusters persist despite titration.  
- Migraine: Maintain hydration and sleep hygiene; patient reports triggers include skipped meals. No change to acute analgesia (paracetamol/ibuprofen within standard limits).  
- Follow-up: Review in clinic in 10–12 weeks, sooner if increased frequency or adverse effects. Patient has contact details for the epilepsy nurse specialist for interim support.  

This patient attended with their partner and described brief episodes characterised by sudden behavioural arrest and loss of awareness, sometimes with lip smacking and post-event confusion lasting up to 20 minutes, though occasional events are limited to brief staring without clear post-ictal phase. There have also been two nocturnal events noted by the partner with unclear semiology; tongue biting not reported. No consistent trigger identified. Medication adherence has improved with use of a weekly pill organiser. In the context of ongoing episodes and the diary entry that seizure diary shows two cluster days this month; sizes unrecorded, we agreed on cautious introduction of adjunctive Levetiracetam with close monitoring for mood changes. No red flags for status epilepticus reported. I reiterated the importance of prompt emergency assessment if a seizure lasts more than five minutes or clusters do not resolve.  

Yours sincerely

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states: "Seizure diary shows two cluster days this month; sizes unrecorded." This provides a numeric frequency of cluster days within a month (two), but does not specify how many seizures occurred within each cluster (cluster size unknown). There is no other quantified frequency given. Therefore, per rules, report frequency as clusters per month with unknown per cluster.
